task: I’m looking at a contract addendum that has a few vague terms and a couple of promises that sound better than they read. Can you help me figure out what I should verify before I sign anything, and what wording I should be wary of?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I can help you sanity-check it.

Before you sign, I’d verify these things:

- **Who is bound**: make sure the addendum clearly names the exact parties and matches the main contract.
- **What it changes**: it should say exactly which sections of the original contract are being modified, added, or replaced.
- **Dates and timing**: confirm effective date, deadlines, delivery dates, renewal dates, and any “within X days” language.
- **Conditions attached to promises**: watch for promises that only apply if something happens first, or if they decide it’s “reasonable,” “practicable,” or “subject to availability.”
- **Definitions**: vague words like “timely,” “reasonable,” “best efforts,” “material,” or “as needed” should be defined or made more specific.
- **Payment terms**: look for hidden fees, automatic increases, offsets, rebates, credits, or vague language about “additional charges.”
- **Termination / cancellation**: check whether either side can exit early, and what notice is required.
- **Remedies and limits**: see whether the addendum narrows your rights, limits liability, or says the new promise is your “sole remedy.”
- **Priority clause**: confirm whether the addendum overrides the main contract if there’s a conflict.
- **Signatures**: make sure the right people are signing and any referenced exhibits or attachments are included.

Wording I’d be wary of:

- “**Subject to**”  
- “**May**” instead of “shall” or “will”
- “**At our sole discretion**”
- “**Reasonable efforts**” / “best efforts” without specifics
- “**Approximate**,” “**estimated**,” or “**anticipated**”
- “**As determined by us**”
- “**Notwithstanding anything to the contrary**” if it’s used to quietly override protections
- “**For convenience**” termination language
- “**In our judgment**” or “if we deem necessary”
- “**Including but not limited to**” when the list is supposed to be complete

A good rule: if a promise sounds important but has no clear deadline, standard, or consequence for nonperformance, it may not be as strong as it looks.

If you want, paste the exact wording of the vague parts and I’ll help you flag what to clarify before signing.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right lane. But it’s still a little generic.

I need the actual wording before I can tell you what’s soft and what’s just normal contract language. The stuff I’d watch hardest:

- **“reasonable” / “commercially reasonable”** — who decides that
- **“best efforts”** — usually fuzzy unless they define it
- **“subject to availability”** — can kill the promise outright
- **“may” vs “shall”** — huge difference
- **“as needed” / “if required” / “at our discretion”** — leaves them room to do nothing
- **“estimated” / “anticipated” / “target”** — not a commitment
- **“sole remedy”** — can box you in
- **“notwithstanding”** — can override protections if buried wrong
- **“time is of the essence”** — matters if they’re strict on you but loose on themselves

Also verify:

- exact section number being changed
- whether the addendum overrides the main agreement or just supplements it
- any fee increase, renewal trap, or auto-extension
- who signs and whether authority is actually there
- any attachment/exhibit referenced but missing

If you paste the vague clauses, I’ll mark them line by line: what’s safe, what needs definition, what’s a red flag.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — paste the exact wording and I’ll mark it line by line.

What I’d look at first:

- **“reasonable” / “commercially reasonable”** — fine sometimes, but weak if there’s no standard
- **“best efforts”** — vague unless the contract defines what that means
- **“subject to availability”** — can make the promise optional
- **“may”** — usually not a commitment; **“shall”** or **“will”** is stronger
- **“at our discretion” / “if required” / “as needed”** — gives them room to do little or nothing
- **“estimated” / “anticipated” / “target”** — not a firm promise
- **“sole remedy”** — can limit what you can do if they don’t perform
- **“notwithstanding”** — can quietly override other protections
- **“time is of the essence”** — important if deadlines only apply to you

Before signing, verify:

- the **exact section(s)** being changed
- whether the addendum **overrides** the main agreement or only adds to it
- any **fees, renewals, extensions, or auto-renewal language**
- any **deadline** or condition tied to the promise
- who is signing and whether they have **authority**
- any referenced **exhibits/attachments** are actually included

If you paste the clauses, I’ll tell you what reads normal, what’s soft, and what I’d want clarified.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Still need the actual clauses, otherwise it’s just theory.

Paste the exact addendum language and I’ll mark it straight:
- what’s normal contract wording
- what’s soft / nonbinding
- what needs to be tightened
- what I’d push back on before signing

If it’s long, just send the sections with the vague terms. That’s enough.

turns.agent_response:
Exactly — paste the actual clauses and I’ll go line by line.

I’ll flag:
- what’s standard contract language
- what’s soft or nonbinding
- what needs a definition or deadline
- what’s a red flag / worth pushing back on

If it’s long, just send the specific sections with the vague wording. That’s enough to work with.

user_simulator.text:
Here’s a realistic chunk of it, cleaned up but close enough to the way it reads:

- “Supplier will use commercially reasonable efforts to accommodate Buyer’s requested changes, subject to availability and operational constraints.”
- “Delivery dates are estimated only and may be adjusted as needed.”
- “Any credits, replacements, or service adjustments will be provided at Supplier’s sole discretion.”
- “This addendum supplements the Agreement and controls only to the extent of any direct conflict.”
- “Buyer acknowledges that time is not of the essence unless expressly stated in writing.”
- “Supplier may terminate this addendum for convenience upon 30 days’ notice.”
- “Any additional charges, if required, will be invoiced in accordance with Supplier’s then-current rates.”
- “The parties will act in good faith to resolve any issues arising under this addendum.”

That’s the kind of language I’m looking at. Some of it smells harmless, some of it looks like they left themselves a lot of room. Line it up the way you would if you were marking what needs tightening.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s how I’d mark it, line by line, in the way I’d want to see it before signing.

## 1) “Supplier will use commercially reasonable efforts to accommodate Buyer’s requested changes, subject to availability and operational constraints.”

**Risk level:** medium

**What it means:**  
This sounds helpful, but it’s soft. They’re only promising “commercially reasonable efforts,” and then they immediately add two escape hatches: **availability** and **operational constraints**.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Define what counts as a “requested change”
- Define who decides whether something is “commercially reasonable”
- Ask what “availability” means in practice
- Ask whether they can refuse for any operational reason, or only for specific listed reasons
- If this matters, require a response deadline and a written approval/rejection process

**Why it’s soft:**  
They can say they tried, but still do nothing.

---

## 2) “Delivery dates are estimated only and may be adjusted as needed.”

**Risk level:** high

**What it means:**  
This is basically **not a firm delivery commitment**. “Estimated only” and “may be adjusted as needed” gives them a lot of room.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Replace “estimated only” with an actual delivery date or window
- Define what triggers an adjustment
- Require notice before any change
- Ask whether you get any remedy if they miss the date
- If timing matters, make the date binding

**Why it’s soft:**  
It reads like a schedule, but legally it behaves more like a target.

---

## 3) “Any credits, replacements, or service adjustments will be provided at Supplier’s sole discretion.”

**Risk level:** high

**What it means:**  
This is very one-sided. If something goes wrong, they get to decide whether you get anything at all.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Ask for objective criteria for when a credit/replacement/adjustment is due
- Replace “sole discretion” with a defined remedy structure
- Clarify whether this is the exclusive remedy or just one option
- Ask for a timeline for issuing any approved credit or replacement

**Why it’s a red flag:**  
“sole discretion” is usually where meaningful customer remedies go to die.

---

## 4) “This addendum supplements the Agreement and controls only to the extent of any direct conflict.”

**Risk level:** medium

**What it means:**  
This is a standard priority clause, but the phrase **“direct conflict”** can be argued over. If they want the addendum to override only narrowly, they may use that wording to keep the main agreement alive in other places.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Confirm whether the addendum is meant to **replace**, **modify**, or merely **supplement**
- Make sure it says the addendum controls over the Agreement **and any conflicting exhibit/order form/policy**
- Confirm there isn’t another clause elsewhere that says the opposite
- Check whether “direct conflict” is too narrow for your situation

**Why it matters:**  
If there’s ambiguity, they may argue the old contract still governs anything not expressly contradicted.

---

## 5) “Buyer acknowledges that time is not of the essence unless expressly stated in writing.”

**Risk level:** medium to high, depending on the deal

**What it means:**  
This weakens timing obligations. If they miss a deadline, it may be harder to argue the date was material unless it was separately written as such.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- If timing matters, make specific dates “time is of the essence”
- Identify which obligations need strict deadlines
- Ask whether late performance creates any remedy
- Make sure they can’t use this clause to excuse delay after the fact

**Why it’s important:**  
If you care about delivery or performance timing, this clause protects them more than you.

---

## 6) “Supplier may terminate this addendum for convenience upon 30 days’ notice.”

**Risk level:** high

**What it means:**  
They can walk away without cause, as long as they give notice.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Ask whether you also have a termination-for-convenience right
- Ask what happens to pending orders, prepaid amounts, or ongoing obligations
- Check whether termination affects the entire Agreement or only the addendum
- Consider requiring cause-based termination only, or a longer notice period
- Clarify whether any survival obligations remain after termination

**Why it’s a big deal:**  
This can make their promises feel real only until they decide otherwise.

---

## 7) “Any additional charges, if required, will be invoiced in accordance with Supplier’s then-current rates.”

**Risk level:** high

**What it means:**  
This is a pricing trap unless the contract tightly defines what “additional charges” are and when they can be imposed.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Define exactly what counts as “additional charges”
- Require prior written approval before any extra charge is incurred
- Ask for a fee schedule or cap
- Clarify whether “then-current rates” means rates in effect at invoice time, order time, or service time
- Check for unilateral rate increases

**Why it’s soft / risky:**  
“if required” plus “then-current rates” can turn into open-ended billing.

---

## 8) “The parties will act in good faith to resolve any issues arising under this addendum.”

**Risk level:** low to medium

**What it means:**  
This is common and usually harmless, but it’s also vague. It sounds nice without creating a concrete obligation.

**What to verify / tighten:**
- Ask whether there’s a required escalation process
- Add timeframes for notice, response, and resolution
- Clarify whether unresolved issues go to mediation, arbitration, or litigation
- Make sure “good faith” doesn’t replace actual performance obligations

**Why it’s mostly harmless:**  
It’s a general cooperation clause, but it shouldn’t be the only thing standing between you and a remedy.

---

# What I’d flag most strongly
If I were marking this for pushback, I’d focus first on:

1. **“estimated only and may be adjusted as needed”**  
2. **“sole discretion”** for credits/replacements/service adjustments  
3. **“may terminate for convenience”**  
4. **“then-current rates”** for additional charges  
5. **“commercially reasonable efforts”** plus broad exceptions  
6. **“time is not of the essence”** if timing matters to you

# What I’d ask them to clarify in writing
- What exactly counts as a “requested change”
- What “availability and operational constraints” means
- Whether delivery dates are binding or just targets
- Who decides if a credit/replacement is owed
- Whether additional charges require prior approval
- Whether termination affects open orders or prepaid amounts
- Whether the addendum overrides conflicting terms in the main agreement, exhibits, and policies

# My quick read
This addendum is **not outrageous**, but it is **written to preserve their flexibility**. The biggest issue is that several clauses sound like commitments while actually functioning as **escape valves**.

If you want, I can do a second pass and rewrite each clause into:
- **buyer-friendly**
- **balanced**
- or **seller-friendly but cleaner**

That usually makes the weak spots obvious fast.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the kind of markup I needed. Not perfect, but it actually separates the soft spots from the normal boilerplate.

The worst ones are the ones I’d circle too:
- **estimated only / may be adjusted as needed**
- **sole discretion** on credits or replacements
- **may terminate for convenience**
- **then-current rates**
- **commercially reasonable efforts** with loose carveouts
- **time is not of the essence** if timing matters

And yeah, the priority clause is the kind of thing people skim and then get burned later if it’s worded too narrowly.

If you can do the rewrite pass, I’d want:
- buyer-friendly
- balanced
- seller-friendly but tighter

That’d be useful. I can compare the wording side by side and see what I’d actually push back on.
